title: St. Simon the Stylite — St. Achilles of Larissa & St. Cassius of Clermont
primary_saint: St. Simon the Stylite the Elder, Master of the Pillar, St. Achilles of Larissa the Bishop-Confessor, and St. Cassius of Clermont the Ascetic Bishop
narrative:
On the fifteenth of Ginbot, the holy Church commemorates the holy, venerable, and wonderworking Simon the Stylite the Elder — the Syrian monk who in the early fifth century invented, or more precisely was driven by the pressure of pilgrims into, the form of asceticism that bears his name: the life of the pillar. Having withdrawn from a monastery because his austerities were deemed excessive, having tried successively more remote forms of solitary life and found each one invaded by the crowds who came seeking healing and counsel, Simon finally mounted a pillar — first low, then progressively higher, until it stood some eighteen metres — and lived on its platform for thirty-seven years, never descending, exposed to the Syrian sun and the Syrian winter, surrounded at the base by thousands of pilgrims from across the known world, becoming paradoxically the most visited and the most visible of all the desert fathers by attempting to be the most inaccessible.

The theology of the pillar, in the Orthodox tradition, is a theology of vertical orientation: Simon's ascent from the ground is the spatial enactment of the soul's ascent from the earthly to the divine, and his permanent elevation above the crowds is not a statement of superiority but of direction, a visual declaration that the Christian life is fundamentally oriented upward, toward God, and that this orientation must be maintained even — especially — when surrounded by the pressing, demanding horizontal world of human need. The crowds did not diminish his prayer; his prayer did not isolate him from the crowds: he prayed twice a day standing with arms outstretched in the form of the Cross, and between these prayers he counselled, healed, arbitrated disputes, dictated letters to the emperor and to the Pope, and received the entire world from the height of his pillar. The pillar is not an escape from engagement but a form of engagement that refuses to be consumed by it.

Saint Achilles of Larissa is the great discovery of this day and a figure of particular importance for the theology of the episcopal office: a bishop of Larissa in Thessaly who was present at the Council of Nicaea, who was known in his lifetime as a wonder-worker and healer, and whose death was followed by such an outpouring of popular veneration that his body was enshrined and became for centuries the most important object of pilgrimage in northern Greece. The tradition preserves a detail about Achilles that captures his character: that when a woman came to him carrying a sick child and he did not have time to pray over the child properly because of the press of other business, he handed the child back and told her to pray herself — that her prayer was as valid as his. This gesture, coming from a bishop who was widely understood to have extraordinary gifts of healing, is the tradition's statement about the nature of episcopal authority: it exists not to concentrate grace in the bishop but to distribute it, to tell the ordinary faithful that they, too, are capable of prayer that reaches God.

Saint Cassius of Clermont brings to this assembly the figure of the Western ascetic bishop — bishop of Clermont in Gaul in the sixth century, contemporary with Gregory of Tours, remembered in the Gallic tradition as a man whose personal austerity was as demanding as any Eastern monk's while his pastoral engagement was as total as any urban bishop's. He fasted continuously, wore coarse clothing, slept minimally, gave away everything that came to him, and spent his days in a perpetual round of liturgy and care for the poor. Gregory of Tours, who knew him personally, records that Cassius died at the altar during the celebration of the Eucharist — collapsed while presiding, and did not rise again. His feast alongside the stylite on his pillar and the bishop who gave away his healing is the tradition's triad on the forms of the ascetic episcopal vocation: height, generosity, and the final oblation of one's own body at the altar of service. Amen.
